coredata.dc:title: Experimental and theoretical studies of a novel electrochemical sensor based on molecularly imprinted polymer and B, N, F-CQDs/AgNPs for enhanced specific identification and dual signal amplification in highly selective and ultra-trace bisphenol S determination in plastic products
coredata.dc:creator: Yao, Jun
Chen, Min
Li, Nannan
Liu, Chaohui
Yang, Mei
coredata.dc:description: As an ideal alternative to bisphenol A (BPA), bisphenol S (BPS) has similar biotoxicity, teratogenicity, carcinogenicity and mutagenicity as BPA. Nevertheless, to date, a fast, sensitive and portable method for meeting on-site measurement of BPS had not been established. Hence, it was particularly urged to develop a fast and highly sensitive method for tracing BPS. Currently, a novel molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) electrochemical sensor had been successfully fabricated for the detection of BPS, wherein the composite of three-doped carbon quantum dots (B, N, F-CQDs) and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) were utilized as an electron conducting layer, and MIP was applied as recognition element of target molecules. The step-by-step fabrication process and the adsorption capacity of the modified electrode were evaluated by cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and chronocoulometry (CC). The results indicated that the synergy between B, N, F-CQDs and AgNPs dramatically improved the sensitivity of the electrode and achieved the amplification of the electrical signal. Meanwhile, the electrochemical activities of BPS were explored by CV and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). And, the various parameters relating to the electrochemical kinetic properties of BPS were calculated. To the best of our knowledge, this was rarely reported in peer journals. The MIP remarkably improved the selectivity of the sensor owing to the specific recognition of the imprinted cavities. The linear response range of the new sensor was 1 × 10−8 M to 5 × 10−5 M with a detection limit of 1.12 × 10−8 M and the electrode designed in this paper could meet the requirement of trace-level measurement of BPS in biological and environmental samples. Additionally, the sensor was used to determine BPS in plastic products with good anti-interference and acceptable recovery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Silver nanoparticles
Signal amplification
Sensing mechanism
Kinetic parameters

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of three-doped carbon quantum dots
Surface morphologies and optical characterization of three doped carbon quantum dots
Fourier transform infrared spectra of three doped carbon quantum dots
X-ray powder diffraction pattern of three doped carbon quantum dots
The surface morphology of silver nanoparticles
The thickness of molecularly imprinted polymer
Surface morphology and roughness of molecularly imprinted polymer
Electrochemical characterization of different modified electrodes
The effect of the ratio of monomer to template
The effect of extraction time for template
The effect of incubation time for target
Stability, repeatability, reusability and recovery experiment

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterization of the B, N, F-CQDs. (A) TEM image of B, N, F-CQDs, (B) UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of B, N, F-CQDs, (C) FT-IR image of B, N, F-CQDs and (D) XRD spectrum of B, N, F-CQDs.
(A) Cyclic voltammogram of electropolymerization pyrrole, SEM (B) and AFM (C) images of MIP.

(A) EIS Nyquist plots and (B) CV of the bare GCE (a), AgNPs/GCE (b), B, N, F-CQDs/AgNPs/GCE (c), MIP-B, N, F-CQDs/AgNPs/GCE before elution (d), and MIP-B, N, F-CQDs/AgNPs/GCE (e). The supporting electrolyte was 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution containing 0.1 M KCl. Scan rate of CV was 50 mV/s. Frequency range of EIS was 0.1 Hz–100 kHZ.
(A) Q–t1/2 plots of the different electrodes in 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution. (B) Q–t1/2 plots of MIP-B, N, F-CQDs/AgNPs/GCE in the CBS (pH 5.0) containing 50 μM BPS.
DPV curves of NIP sensor (a) and MIP sensor (b) in the CBS (pH 5.0) containing 5 μM BPS.

Electrocatalytic performances of the samples. (A) CV of 20 μM BPS on MIP-B, N, F-CQDs/AgNPs/GCE at different scan rates (25, 50, 100, 150, 200, 300, 400 mV/s). (B) The plot for the dependence of peak potential on thelogarithm of scan rate. (C) The DPV curves of different BPS concentrations obtained at MIP-B, N, F-CQDs/AgNPs/GCE in CBS (pH 5.0): 1.0 × 10−8, 5.0 × 10−8, 1.0 × 10−7, 5.0 × 10−7, 1.0 × 10−6, 1.0 × 10−5, 2.0 × 10−5, 3.0 × 10−5, 4.0 × 10−5 and 5.0 × 10−5 mol/L. (D) Calibration plot of peak current versus BPS concentrations. Incr E: 0.02 V, amplitude: 0.06 V, and frequency: 15 Hz.
Effects of (A) template/functional monomer, (B) extraction time, and (C) incubation time to the DPV current response of 10 μM BPS (n = 3).

(A) The DPV of BPS in different pH CBS (pH: 3, 3.6, 4.0, 4.6, 5.0, 5.6, 6, 6.6) at MIP-B, N, F-CQDs/AgNPs/GCE. (B) Plots of the relation between oxidation peak current and solution pH. (C) Plots of the relation between oxidation peak potential and solution pH. Scan rate: 0.05 V/s. (D) The electrochemical reaction process of BPS.

(A) DPV voltammograms of 10 μM BPS and analogues (the equivalent concentration of bisphenol A, sulfanilic acid, hydroquinone, p-nitrophenol, 2,5-diaminophenol), (B) Column graph of DPV signals in BPS and the analogues. (C) Column graph of DPV signals in BPS and 500-fold interfering substance (glucose, L-phenylalanine, L-cysteine, Na+, Cu2+, K+, Zn2+ and NH4 +).
A schematic illustration of the electrode preparation process and the molecular recognition process.
Comparison of different modified electrodes for the determination of BPS.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Platinum nanoparticles-functionalized poly (diallyl dimethyl ammonium chloride)-diamond powder.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of BPS in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Experimental and theoretical studies of a novel electrochemical sensor based on molecularly imprinted polymer and B, N, F-CQDs/AgNPs for enhanced specific identification and dual signal amplification in highly selective and ultra-trace bisphenol S determination in plastic products

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering
Sichuan University of Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu, 610500, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As an ideal alternative to bisphenol A (BPA), bisphenol S (BPS) has similar biotoxicity, teratogenicity, carcinogenicity and mutagenicity as BPA. Nevertheless, to date, a fast, sensitive and portable method for meeting on-site measurement of BPS had not been established. Hence, it was particularly urged to develop a fast and highly sensitive method for tracing BPS. Currently, a novel molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) electrochemical sensor had been successfully fabricated for the detection of BPS, wherein the composite of three-doped carbon quantum dots (B, N, F-CQDs) and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) were utilized as an electron conducting layer, and MIP was applied as recognition element of target molecules. The step-by-step fabrication process and the adsorption capacity of the modified electrode were evaluated by cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and chronocoulometry (CC). The results indicated that the synergy between B, N, F-CQDs and AgNPs dramatically improved the sensitivity of the electrode and achieved the amplification of the electrical signal. Meanwhile, the electrochemical activities of BPS were explored by CV and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). And, the various parameters relating to the electrochemical kinetic properties of BPS were calculated. To the best of our knowledge, this was rarely reported in peer journals. The MIP remarkably improved the selectivity of the sensor owing to the specific recognition of the imprinted cavities. The linear response range of the new sensor was 1 × 10−8 M to 5 × 10−5 M with a detection limit of 1.12 × 10−8 M and the electrode designed in this paper could meet the requirement of trace-level measurement of BPS in biological and environmental samples. Additionally, the sensor was used to determine BPS in plastic products with good anti-interference and acceptable recovery.

ce:section.ce:para: Bisphenol A (BPA) [2,2-bis (4-hydroxyphenyl) propane (C15H16O2)] is an important organic chemical raw material, which is everywhere, from polycarbonate water bottles, packaging materials to the resin lining of beverage cans [1,2]. Since BPA is an environmental hormone substance that arouses abnormal hormone activity, it engenders irreversible damage to living organisms and the environment, which has been confirmed by a large number of studies [3–5]. For these reasons, the production and application of products based on BPA has been limited, which has prompted industry to seek alternative chemicals. Bisphenol S (BPS) [(bis-(4-hydroxyphenyl) sulfone (C12H10O4S)], a substitute for BPA, has been widely used in BPA-free products. In recent years, BPS contaminants have been detected in plastic products, various water environments, and even human blood and neonatal cord blood [6–8]. As to BPS, which has a longer biological half-life and stronger skin permeability than BPA, it gives rise to a greater physical burden and impairs human health. In addition, BPS may pose a potential hazard to the ecological environment due to its difficulty in degradation and ease of enrichment [9]. Moreover, recent studies have found that BPS also has endocrine disrupting effects which have a negative impact on human health, and owing to the methyl groups of its two phenol rings are replaced by sulfone groups, in some terms of toxicity, the effect is even stronger than BPA [10].

Up to now, the methods of detecting BPS are not as common as that of detecting BPA, which mainly depend on chromatographic analysis, such as P-GC-MS [11], LC–ESI–MS [12], UHPLC–MS/MS [13,14] and SPE–UPLC–MS [15,16]. Although these methods have higher accuracy and sensitivity for quantifying estrogen BPS, they involve complex sample preparation, toxic organic agents, higher analytical costs, time-consuming and difficult to handle, which limits the widespread use of such technologies [17]. In contrast, electrochemical detection method attracts much attention for its simple, sensitive, cheap and microminiaturization for constant testing [18]. However, electrode typically is not provided with specifically recognizing ability. Modifying the molecularly imprinted materials on the electrode surface is a good solution to the problem. Molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) is provided with the ability to specifically select, adsorb and bond template molecules, and has been widely used in various selective separation, enrichment and detection [19,20]. Combining molecular imprinting technique with electrochemical sensors to develop new MIP sensors is an important developing direction of new electrochemical sensors [21,22].

In recent years, nanomaterials have gradually attracted people's attention and are widely applied in various fields, especially in sensing [23–26]. Various methods and nanomaterials have been employed to construct the interfacial activity of electrochemical sensors, and the characteristics of nanomaterials (i.e. large specific surface area, excellent chemical reactivity, high catalytic efficiency, strong adsorption capacity, etc.) have been fully utilized to improve the performance of various aspects of sensors [27–29]. The development of nanomaterials and electrochemistry has provided a new opportunity for each other. Silver is probably one of the most conductive materials on the planet. Nano-silver materials are endowed with unique physical and chemical properties, like small volume, large specific surface area, high heat transfer conductivity, biocompatibility, antibacterial property and catalytic properties, etc. [30–33], which have led to their tremendous development in the field of electrochemical sensors. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new type of nanomaterials developing in recent years. CQDs are novel nano-carbon materials with a very small size (below 10 nm) and have a series of amazing advantages, such as excellent physicochemical properties and biocompatibility, good optical properties, high water solubility, low toxicity, remarkable electrical conductivity and wide raw materials, which have become among the latest frontiers of the research on electrochemical sensing [34–37].

In this study, three-doped carbon quantum dots (B, N, F-CQDs) as the electrode modification material were prepared by microwave using ionic liquid and citric acid. The chemical structures of B, N, F-CQDs were characterized by FTIR, IR, TEM, UV-vis and fluorescence spectrophotometer. The BF and CN bonds assigned on the spectrum confirmed that the B, N, and F atoms were successfully decorated on CQDs. And a convenient, highly sensitive and selective electrochemical sensor integrating with MIP and electrochemical technology for the determination of BPS was proposed. The overall procedures were shown in Scheme 1 . The electrochemical sensor based on MIP was fabricated by electrochemical polymerization of BPS and pyrrole on B, N, F-CQDs and AgNPs composite modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE), wherein pyrrole served as the functional monomer, BPS as the template monomer. The template monomer was extracted afterwards to create the imprinted cavities that were complementary to the template in the polymer matrix. The BPS could be specifically identified and bound by the imprinted cavities. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the linear response range for BPS concentrations by the sensor was 1 × 10−8 M to 5 × 10−5 M with the detection limit as low as 1.12 × 10−8 M (S/N = 3), showing a wider detection range and lower detection limits comparing with the reported electrochemical methods for the detection of BPS. Furthermore, the MIP-B, N, F-CQDs/AgNPs/GCE sensor effectively recognized BPS from various interfering substances. It was observed that the sensor developed had the superiorities of rapid electrochemical response, high sensitivity, marvelous selectivity, stability and reusability, and was also suitable for the analysis of BPS in plastic products with satisfactory anti-interference ability and acceptable recovery.

Bisphenol S (C12H10O4S), lithium perchlorate trihydrate (LiClO4·3H2O), pyrrole (C4H5N), sulfanilic acid (C6H7NO3S), p-nitrophenol (C6H5NO3) and 1-methy l-3-butylimidotetrafluoroborate ([BMIM]BF4) were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. and 2′5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid (C6H8N2O3S) was ordered from Energy Chemical Co., Ltd. Other chemical reagents were obtained from Kelong chemical reagent Co., Ltd. All chemical reagents were analytical grade chemicals which were used without further purification. The electrochemical measurements were implemented in citric acid buffer solution (0.1 M) which was prepared by mixing 0.1 M citric acid (C6H8O7) and 0.1 M trisodium citrate dihydrate (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O). Ultrapure water was freshly prepared.

A WQF-520 FTIR spectrometer (Beijing Rayleigh Analytical Instruments Co., Ltd.) was employed to perform the IR characterization of B, N, F-CQDs and the wave number ranged from 400 to 4000 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the B, N, F-CQDs was collected utilizing X-ray Powder diffractometer (PANalytical B.V., Netherlands). The surface structure of B, N, F-CQDs and the surface morphology of AgNPs were examined using transmission electron microscopes (TEM) (Libra 200FE 200 kV, Carl Zeiss SMT Pte. Ltd.) and field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) (FEI Quanta 650 FEG, FEI Czech Republic S.r.o), respectively. The fluorescence spectrum was recorded by a Perkin Elmer LS55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer Instrument Co., Ltd.). The UV–vis absorption spectrum was performed on a UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu (China) Co., Ltd.). A scanning electron microscope (SEM) (ZEISS EV0 MA15, Carl Zeiss Co., Ltd.) and an atomic force microscope (AFM) (NT-MDT Prima; Bruker Dimension Edge, Shanghai NTI Co., Ltd.) was used to record the surface morphology and roughness of MIP. Cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and chronocoulometry (CC) were completed using a CHI 650E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai ChenHua Instruments Co., Ltd.) at room temperature. A traditional three-electrode system was employed for all electrochemical experiments, where a bare or modified GCE, saturated calomel electrode and Pt wire served as the working, reference and counter electrodes, respectively. The pH measurements were performed on a pHS-3C exact digital pH meter (Shanghai ChenHua Instruments Co., Ltd.).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of three-doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: B, N, F-CQDs were successfully prepared by one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis. The specific method to achieve was as follows: 50 mL porcelain crucible containing citric acid monohydrate (2 mmol) and [BMIM]BF4 (6 mmol) was placed in a microwave oven and heated for 42 s. The obtained brownish-yellow product was diluted with 30 mL ethanol, and then centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 50 min to remove agglomerated carbon. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane, and then dialyzed through a dialysis membrane (MWCO of 1000) for 24 h to remove residual unreacted small molecules. Finally, B, N, F-CQDs were obtained by further freeze-drying the solution.

Prior to the modification, the GCE (5 mm) was polished to a mirror-like surface by using Al2O3 powders with a particle size of 1, 0.3 and 0.05 μm. Subsequently, the polished electrode was cleaned sequentially by sonication for 5 min in nitric acid (1:1, v/v), ethanol and distilled water to remove impurities on the electrode surface.

The above electrode was subjected to activation treatment in 0.5 M H2SO4 between −0.2 and 1.3 V at 100 mV/s until the CV sweeps being steady. And the AgNPs/GCE was prepared by the method that had been reported [38]. Briefly, the pretreated electrode was placed in a solution consisting of 3 mM silver nitrate and 0.1 M potassium nitrate at a constant potential of 0.2 V for 90 s. Next, 10 μL ethanol solution of B, N, F-CQDs was added dropwise to the electrode surface and dried at room temperature for 6 h before further modification of the electrode by molecular imprinting technique.

Firstly, the electrode obtained in the previous step was immersed in acetonitrile solution containing 0.1 M LiClO4, 40 mM pyrrole and 10 mM BPS for 10 min. A printed polymer membrane, in which BPS molecules were embedded, was obtained by repeated CV with a scanning speed of 50 mV/s and a potential range of −0.2–0.8 V. Following, most of the template molecules were removed from the polypyrrole membrane by immersing electrode in methanol-acetic acid (v/v = 9/1) solution and gently stirring for 15 min. Then, to elute the template molecules more thoroughly, quickly and efficiently, an electrochemical elution method was selected. That is, the polypyrrole membrane was peroxidized by CV, and the template molecules in the polymerized membrane were removed by electrostatic repulsion between the functional molecules and the template molecules. Finally, a bisphenol S-imprinted polypyrrole molecularly imprinted sensor (MIP-B,N,F-CQDs/AgNPs/GCE) with molecular recognition capabilities was obtained.

Mineral water bottles and disposable paper cups were purchased from the local supermarket (Daronghua, China), and disposable dropper was purchased from Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd. These samples were cut into small pieces with scissors and washed twice with ultrapure water. And then, the sample (about 1 g) was placed in a beaker containing 30 mL ethanol at 50 °C for 4 h.

The CV employed to characterize the electrode modification process was implemented over a potential range from −0.2 V to 0.6 V at a scan rate of 50 mV/s in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution. EIS was performed between 0.1 Hz and 100 kHz. CC was carried out in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- or 0.1 M citrate buffer solution (CBS, pH 5.0) containing 20 μM BPS. DPV was used to evaluate the electrochemical behavior of BPS on the electrode surface with the potential range from 0.2 to 1.3 V in the CBS (pH 5.0). The pulse amplitude was 50 mV, the pulse width was 0.06 s, and the pulse period was 0.5 s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphologies and optical characterization of three doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphology of B, N, F-CQDs was characterized by TEM. As could be seen from Fig. 1 A, the size of the B, N, F-CQDs was very small, about 2–5 nm, which was mainly attributed to the fact that these interaction forces, the large electrostatic repulsion and steric hindrance between the B, N, F-CQDs surface groups, greatly limited the growth of B, N, F-CQDs [39]. As shown in Fig. 1B, the optical property of B, N, F-CQDs was investigated. The excitation and emission spectra (Fig. 1B) indicated that the B, N, F-CQDs ethanol solution emitted blue fluorescence at 443 nm under 375 nm excitation.
In order to assess whether the B,N,F-CQDs had been successfully prepared, techniques, such as FT-IR, XRD, were utilized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fourier transform infrared spectra of three doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Infrared spectroscopy (Fig. 1C) confirmed that the surface of B, N, F-CQDs contained various groups. The strong and broad peak of B, N, F-CQDs and ionic liquids at 1066 cm−1 was mainly attributed to the stretching vibration of the BF bond of the ionic liquid. The absorption peaks at 1380 cm−1 and 1172 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of CN and CO bonds, respectively [40]. The absorption peak at 1723 cm−1 was caused by the C=O vibration in the COOH group. The infrared spectra of B, N, F-CQDs had obvious absorption peaks at 1576 cm−1 and 1457 cm−1, chiefly thanks to the stretching vibrations of C=N and C=C in [BMIM][BF4] molecules [41]. The absorption peak at 2967 cm−1 was produced by the skeleton vibration of the imidazole aromatic ring and the stretching vibration of C=C. The absorption peak at 3415 cm−1 was generated by the stretching vibration of the hydroxyl group at the edge of the synthesized B, N, F-CQDs. In summary, from the whole FT-IR spectrum, it could be concluded that citric acid decomposed during the thermal polymerization process. On the contrary, B, N, F-CQDs can be functionalized by using [BMIM][BF4] as the surface modifier [42] due to the high thermal stability of it.

ce:section.ce:section-title: X-ray powder diffraction pattern of three doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD pattern of B, N, F-CQDs showed a broad peak centered at 22.3°, which was consistent with the (002) crystal plane of graphite. The interlayer distance of B, N, F-CQDs (0.40 nm) was much larger than that of graphite (0.34 nm) [43]. In general, an atom with great electronegative was introduced in the graphite skeleton structure, which could reinforce the interlayer attraction and make the pitch smaller. According to the literature, such phenomenon was also present in oxygen-doped g-C3N4 [44]. It was believed that the atomic layers of g-C3N4 would be subjected to stronger interaction forces and result in a shortening of the interfacial distance, since oxygen with higher electronegativity was incorporated into the skeleton, supplanting the original nitrogen atoms. Based on the above knowledge, it could be known that if fluorine was present in the framework structure of graphite, the interlayer spacing would be reduced because fluorine had stronger electronegativity and electron-withdrawing ability than carbon. Therefore, the F atoms of B, N, F-CQDs were not doped in the skeleton, but were located at the edge such that the interlayer attraction became weak and the pitch increased. On the other hand, an oxygen-containing functional group at the edge of B, N, F-CQDs, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, etc., further increased the layer spacing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The surface morphology of silver nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphology of AgNPs was observed by field emission scanning electronmicroscopy and is shown in Fig. 2 . A plenty of spherical particles with a particle diameter of 50–100 nm are uniformly dispersed on the surface of the electrode, which indicated that the AgNPs had been successfully prepared. And some larger particles were caused by agglomeration which was attributed to the large specific surface area and small size effect of the AgNPs. The AgNPs dispersed on the surface of the electrode can effectively increase the surface area of the electrode, accelerate the electron transfer rate, and improve the detection sensitivity of the sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The thickness of molecularly imprinted polymer
ce:section.ce:para:
CV was used to record the electropolymerization of pyrrole in the presence of template molecules. Fig. 3 A suggested that as the current intensity decreased, the weakly conductive polymer film covered the electrode surface layer by layer and resulting in a weakening of the volt-ampere response. The thickness of the polymer film can be estimated by the following equation [45]:
(1)
d = m Q / F A ρ
where d is the thickness of the polymer film, m is the molecular weight of functional monomers, F is the Faraday constant, A is the surface area of electrode and ρ is the density of the polypyrrole. Q is the quantity of electric charge consumed during electropolymerization and can be obtained by integrating voltammogram, Q = 3.72 × 10−3 C. And assuming the density of polypyrrole was similar with that of pyrrole, the film thickness was about 136.3 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology and roughness of molecularly imprinted polymer
ce:section.ce:para: It could be seen from Fig. 3B that the surface of the MIP was rough and contained a plurality of cavities with uniform pore diameter, which indicated that the template molecules were eluted smoothly, and these cavities facilitated the binding of BPS, resulting in an excellent selectivity of the constructed electrode. As we all know, AFM can provide a simple method for calculating the roughness of materials. Through the analysis of AFM data [46], the roughness of the MIP was calculated to be 4.89 nm, and the loose surface structure was beneficial to the re-identification of BPS.

MIP could specifically identify target molecules, and its effects was comparable to natural biomolecular recognition systems, such as enzymes and substrates, antigens and antibodies, etc. So, it had a broad application prospect. Scheme 1. showed the preparation process of new electrode, and its highly selective recognition properties in the presence of structurally similar interferents. AgNPs and B, N, F-CQDs magnified the signal of detecting BPS by accelerating the electron transfer rate of the electrode. The BPS-imprinted polymer was immobilized on the electrode as a sensor identification element, by the electrochemical polymerization of pyrrole monomers in the presence of template molecules. As can be seen, initially, the hydroxyl groups and carbonyl groups presenting on the template molecule interacted with the amino groups on the functional molecule to form hydrogen bonding, which allowed the molecular template complex to be electrical aggregated on the surface of B, N, F-CQDs/AgNPs/GCE. During the process of CV, pyrrole lost an electron to form a radical cation. Then, the radical cations undergone self-polymerization accompanying by the loss of the two protons, to form a dimer that was more susceptible to oxidation. Following, the dimer continued to be oxidized to form a radical cation, which reacted with the monomer/dimer to form a trimer/tetramer. Then, a series of similar reactions, the chain-coupled reaction between radical cation and oligomeric radical cations, occur continuously until a long-chain polymer (polypyrrole) was formed [47]. The polymerization mechanism of pyrrole was shown in Fig. 4 . And then, the electrode was immersed in a mixed solution of methanol and glacial acetic acid with constant agitation to achieve the purpose of leaching out the target molecules. The removal of the target molecules left a three-dimensional hole on the polymer skeleton, which could perfectly match the shape and size of the template molecules and contained functional groups that specifically bound to the imprinted molecules. These specifically identified pores could assist in selectively rebinding of the target molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of different modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The surface modification of electrode was characterized by EIS and CV in 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution.

EIS measurements provided some information about changes in electrode surface impedance. The dynamical information of Faraday process could be obtained in the high frequency region. While, the information about the substance diffuses of the electrode surface could be known from the low frequency region. The diameter of the semicircle in the Nyquist plot was equal to the electron transfer resistance, Rct, of the substance [48]. Comparing the Nyquist plots of the bare electrode and the different modified electrodes in Fig. 5 A, it was concluded that the radius of B, N, F-CQDs/AgNPs/GCE was the smallest and the slope of the straight line was the highest, which indicated that the electrode jointly decorated by AgNPs and B, N, F-CQDs could greatly reduce the electron transfer resistance and significantly improve the diffusion rate of the probe molecule due to the excellent conductivity and electrocatalytic properties of the B, N, F-CQDs and AgNPs. When the MIP was modified on B, N, F-CQDs/AgNPs/GCE, the resistance increased observably, as shown in curve d, which was attributed to the shielding effect of the polypyrrole imprinted film. However, after eluting the template molecule, a significant decrease in the diameter of the semicircle was observed, suggesting that the template molecule was eluted, and the resulting pores opened up the electron channel.

The results of the different modification processes which were recorded by CV were presented in Fig. 5B. Curve a showed that the bare GCE had a pair of distinct redox peaks. As the curve b showed, when the AgNPs were deposited on the surface of the GCE, the current of the sensor was significantly increased owing to excellent catalytic properties and specific surface area of it. Meanwhile, based on the excellent electrical conductivity and large specific surface area of B, N, F-CQDs, the current response was further increased when it was coated on the surface of AgNPs/GCE. After the template molecules were eluted, the current response showed a dramatic increase, which attributed to the increase of binding sites on the surface of the electrode, resulting in an enlargement in the effective electrocatalytic surface of the sensor.

The various modified electrodes and bare GCE were immersed in 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution and CC was employed to describe the relationship of Q vs. t. Through the analysis of the experimental data, it could be known that the quantity of electricity had a linear relationship with the square root of the time, just as Fig. 6 described. And the slope of the B, N, F-CQDs/AgNPs/GCE, B, N, F-CQDs/GCE, AgNPs/GCE, bare GCE and MIP-B, N, F-CQDs/AgNPs/GCE was 52.33, 46.97, 37.86, 29.41, 13.41, respectively. Hence, the electroactive surface areas of the electrodes could be calculated with the slope of Q vs. t1/2 according to the Cottrell equation:
(2)
Q = 2 nFAC
( D t )
1 / 2
π
− 1 / 2
+
Q
a d s
+
Q
d l
where n, C and D is the electron transfer number, concentration and diffusion coefficient of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-, respectively, Qdl is double layer charge and Qdas is Faradic charge. The active surface areas for the B, N, F-CQDs/AgNPs/GCE, B, N, F-CQDs/GCE, AgNPs/GCE, bare GCE, MIP-B, N, F-CQDs/AgNPs/GCE was calculated to be 0.348, 0.312, 0.2516, 0.195 and 0.089 cm2, respectively. By comparison, the activated region was the largest after co-modification with AgNPs and B, N, F-CQDs. And, the activation area of the new electrode was only half of the bare electrode, which was mainly attributed to the weak conductivity of the molecularly imprinted film. In addition, the diffusion coefficient of PBS could also be acquired by analyzing the data obtained by the CC in the CBS containing 50 μM BPS. According to this equation Q = 0.36t1/2+0.0073, R2 = 0.997 and Cottrell equation, the diffusion coefficient of PBS was determined to be 1.38 cm2/s.

In order to investigate the feasibility of the sensor's high selectivity to recognize BPS, MIP and non-imprinted polymer (NIP) sensors were employed to detect 5 μM BPS. It was obvious that the NIP sensor's response to the target was very weak and negligible. While, under the same conditions, the MIP sensor response violently to the target, mainly attributing to the imprinted film of BPS contained a quantity of specific bonding sites, which can quickly capture the BPS in the solution and lock it firmly in the cavity, and this recognition capability was converted to an electrical signal output by an electrochemical workstation (see Fig. 7 ).

The CV curves of the new electrode at different scanning speeds were shown in Fig. 8A, which revealed an apparent phenomenon that the peak current increased with the scanning speed increasing. In addition, the peak potential and the logarithm of scan rate showed a good linear relationship, and the fitting equation could be expressed as Ep = 0.024lnν+0.991, with a correlation coefficient of 0.975. The relationship between Ep and ν could be expressed by the following equation [49]:
(3)
E p
=
E θ
+
R T
α n F
ln
RT
k θ
α n F
+
RT αnF
lnν
Where Eθ is the formal potential, n is the electron transfer number, α is the electron transfer coefficient, F is Faraday constant, Kθ is the standard transfer rate constant. T is the temperature (T = 298 K). R is the gas constant (R = 8.314 J/mol·K). The value of the n was easily calculated to be 2.14 ≈ 2.

Under the optimized conditions, the detection range and detection limit of molecularly imprinted sensors were studied by DPV. It could be seen from the Fig. 8C that as the concentration of BPS increased, the oxidation peak current also gradually increased, and there were two good linear relationships in the range of 50 μM–0.01 μM. In the low concentration region (0.01–0.1 μM), the fitting equation was: I = 22.3797C + 7.05164, R2 = 0.997. In the high concentration region (0.5–50 μM), the fitting equation was: I = 0.16295C + 12.3687, R2 = 0.969. Furthermore, the detection limit was calculated as 1.12 × 10−8 mol/L (LOD = 3 N/S). S is the slope of the working curve and N is the standard deviation of ten blank samples tested. In this experiment, since the DPV curve did not show an oxidation peak when the blank sample was measured, the standard deviation was obtained by measuring the low concentration of the spiked sample instead of the blank standard solution multiple times. According to the literature, the minimum concentration of BPS to induce estrogen-dependent gene expression in vitro was 300 nM [50]. Hence, the electrode designed in this paper could meet the requirement of trace level measurement of BPS in biological and environmental samples. Table 1 compared the present work with other reported BPS sensors, which manifested the fabricated sensor showed a larger linear range and lower detection limit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of the ratio of monomer to template
ce:section.ce:para: After electropolymerization, the number of template molecules embedded in the polymer matrices was determined by the ratio of template molecules to functional monomers. In order to explore the influence of the different ratio on the recognition capabilities of imprinted sensor, we optimized it. As shown in Fig. 9 , current response of the MIP sensor reached to the highest when the ratio was 1:4. As the number of template molecules increased, the current response of the sensor decreased correspondingly, which was attributed to the fact that the number of functional monomers was so scarce that the template molecules couldn't be sufficiently integrated with functional monomer pores. When the ratio was lower than 1:4, the excess functional monomer occupied the surface area of the electrode, which reduced the number of recognition sites and affected the effective identification of the target molecules by the constructed sensor. Therefore, a polymerization solution that the concentration ratio of BPS to pyrrole was 1:4 was used for the experiment to prepare a blotting membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of extraction time for template
ce:section.ce:para: Considering that the bisphenol compound was liable to undergo an oxidation reaction on the surface of the electrode [54,55] and the oxidation product adsorbing on the surface of the electrode prevented the BPS from contacting the active sites, the sensitivity of the sensor was reduced. So, sufficiently removing the oxides on the surface was advantageous for improving the recognition ability and reproducibility of the electrode, which was exactly why we explored the different extraction time of the target. As displayed, when the extraction time was extended from 5 min to 15 min, the response current also increased accordingly and reached a maximum at 15 min. Subsequently, along with the increasing of the extraction time, current intensity almost hardly changed. Hence, the optimum extraction time was 15 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of incubation time for target
ce:section.ce:para: The incubation process of template molecules on the polymeric film was an essential factor affecting the detection results. So, for the template molecules, it would take a period of time to enter the imprinted holes and reach the equilibrium. An appropriate incubation time was beneficial to the amplification of the electrochemical response signal of the BPS. As shown in the Fig. 9C, the current response of BPS increased with the increase of incubation time. The obtained results expressed that increasing of incubation time up to 90 s, the effective imprinted holes on the polymeric film were substantially occupied by the BPS. Thereafter, further prolonging the incubation time, not only would the amount of BPS which be bonded not increase, but a small portion of the BPS bonded might be able to further peel off from the surface of the film, and resulting in a slight decrease in the current response. Therefore, this experiment selected 90 s as the optimal adsorption time of BPS.

The pH value of the buffer solution not only affected the electron transfer rate, but also affected the existing form of the target molecule in the solution, which in turn had an effect on the recognition performance of the polymeric film. As shown in Fig. 10 , when the pH of the system was 5.0, the response current reached the maximum. As the pH value of the system continued to increase, the response current decreased. It could be inferred that when the pH of the system was greater than 5, a negative charge was built on the BPS molecules. Since the polypyrrole film which was subjected to peroxidation would produce carbonyl and carboxyl with negative charge, electrostatic repulsion occurred to the BPS molecule, which was disadvantageous for the combination. Therefore, pH 5.0 was chosen as the best optimization result for the detection system.

The oxidation peak potential shifted negatively with increasing pH, which indicated that the deprotonation process of BPS oxidation occurred on the surface of the electrode. And the peak potential had a good linear relationship with pH, and the fitting equation was: Ep = −0.05979 pH + 1.3401, R2 = 0.993. The slope was 59.79 mV/pH. According to the Nernst equation:
E p
＝ E - 59
m n
p H , m/n = 1 was obtained, that is, the electrochemical process was an oxidation process in which the number of protons was equal to the number of electrons. The electrochemical reaction process was shown in the Fig.10D. The electron transfer coefficient (α) can be calculated by the following formula:
(4)
d
E
p
dpH
=
R T m
α n F
n and m are the number of electrons and protons lost in the reaction, respectively. R, T and F represent its common meaning. The value of α was calculated to be 0.43, which was in the range of 0.3–0.7, in accordance with the work written by A. J. Bard and L. F. Faulkner [56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, repeatability, reusability and recovery experiment
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the MIP-B, N, F-CQDs/AgNPs/GCE was tested for more than 10 days. The sensor was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C and used for the determination of BPS with DPV after every 4 days. The results demonstrated that significant current changes were not observed in the 10 μM BPS, and the current intensities were 92.7% and 90% of the initial values respectively, which confirmed the durability of the sensor. Excellent stability and repeatability were essential parts to put research from the laboratory to the practical application. To test the repeatability of the new electrode, six new electrodes were prepared separately by means of the same method and the RSD % was calculated to be 7.13%. And in order to evaluate the reusability of the electrode, one electrode was repeatedly used for detection. and the results of three detection respectively were 12.27 μA, 11.69 μA, 10.92 μA, and RSD% was 5.8%, indicating that the electrode in this experiment could be reused.

To further verify the viability of the newly constructed electrode, a recovery experiment was performed. The satisfied results with the recoveries between 93.6% ∼ 107% and RSD <5.15% were shown in Table 2 , indicating that the method met the requirements of routine analysis. At the same time, the results also showed that it had a good repeatability, high precision and reliable practical value.

In order to analyze and evaluate the feasibility of MIP-B, N, F-CQDs/AgNPs/GCE for the detection of BPS in actual samples, the recovery test of BPS in actual samples was carried out. The content of BPS in the spiked sample was determined according to the standard curve, and the recovery rate of the corresponding experiment was analyzed. From the analysis results (Table 3 ), the recovery range was between 99.3 and 106.5%, indicating that a small amount of impurities in the sample will not interfere with the measurement of the electrode, and the error was within the allowable range (≤5%). At the same time, this not only showed that the established sensing method had good accuracy for the actual sample detection, but also proved that this method could be used for the migration detection of BPS in real food and agricultural products.

Herein, a facile, stable and highly sensitive and selective MIP sensor was constructed by the electrochemistry method for BPS determination. B, N, F-CQDs and AgNPs were used to accelerate electronic transmission and enhance electrode sensitivity. Molecular imprinting technique was employed to improve the specifically recognizing ability of the electrode. Not only had the MIP-B, N, F-CQDs/AgNPs/GCE sensor represented a wide linear concentration range and low detection limit, but also excellent stability, reproducibility and good specificity of detection had come for the new sensor. Furthermore, the proposed method was successfully applied for the analysis of BPS in the real samples with satisfactory recoveries. This simple and selective strategy could be further desired to fabricate various electrochemical sensors for detecting other environmental contaminants and various electroactive species.
The authors have no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51802273), Foundation of Science & Technology Department of Sichuan Province (No. 19YYJC2882), Foundation of Sichuan Educational Committee (No. 14ZB0050), School Technology Fund of Southwest Petroleum University (No. 2013XJZ016), College Students' Innovative Entrepreneurial Training Projects in Sichuan Province (No. 201710615067), College Students' Innovative Entrepreneurial Training Projects in Sichuan Province (No. 201810615090), Key Project of Southwest Petroleum University Open Experiments (No. KSZ17111), Key Project of Southwest Petroleum University Open Experiments (No. KSZ17112), Key Project of Southwest Petroleum University Open Experiments (No. KSZ17083), Ordinary Project of Southwest Petroleum University Open Experiments (No. KSP17086).
